title: The Origin of the Bible Presbyterian Church
speaker: Pastor Tito Lyro
source_files: The Origin of the Bible Presbyterian Church.txt
outline: Why we celebrate being Protestants in October (October 31, 1517)
The origins of the Bible Presbyterian Church
summary: An October Sunday School lesson on the origin of the Bible Presbyterian Church, presented as part of the annual Reformation celebration. Opens with the significance of October 31, 1517 (Luther's 95 Theses), establishes the Protestant heritage, and then traces the specific history that gave rise to the Bible Presbyterian Church—its founding figures, ecclesiastical separations, and confessional commitments.
transcript:
## Our Protestant Heritage

Every October we take the time to celebrate the fact that we are Protestants. We are the original protesters — the ones who stood up to the establishment and brought great change to this world. Why do we do that in October? Because on October 31, 1517, the Augustinian monk Martin Luther posted to the door of his local church in Wittenberg, Germany, 95 reasons why the doctrine of indulgences — a doctrine that had become accepted in the church — was contrary to the Scriptures and contrary to the established dogma of the church. It is nearly universally accepted that this was the official beginning of the Protestant Reformation. That original act of literally nailing paper to the door of the church marks, at least officially, the beginning of the Protestant Reformation.

The Bible Presbyterian Church is an heir of the Protestant Reformation, and we very proudly celebrate our heritage. We stand in the tradition of the Protestant Reformation. In our church we celebrate the Reformation by using our Sunday School time to consider historical events and people leading up to the Reformation and flowing out of it.

This year we are going to stay close to home and consider the events that gave birth to the Bible Presbyterian Church as we stand in that stream that flows from the Protestant Reformation. In this lesson I will focus on the Fundamentalist-Modernist Controversy, because it is that controversy — that struggle — that ultimately gave birth to the Bible Presbyterian Church. In the next lesson, Elder Andrew Hoy will teach us about the embodiment of modernism in the person of Harry Emerson Fosdick. Then Elder Nick Anderson will follow with a lesson on the champion of the fundamentalists, J. Gresham Machen. And we are very thankful that we can end this series in October with Dr. John Battle — a professor of New Testament at Westminster Reformed Seminary and a long-time Bible Presbyterian minister — teaching us about the most widely known Bible Presbyterian minister in our history, the Reverend Dr. Carl McIntyre.

## The Roots of the Controversy

In considering the Fundamentalist-Modernist Controversy, it is difficult to decide how far back in history to trace its beginning. One could go all the way back to Genesis 3 and the prophesied struggle between the seed of the woman and the serpent — because that is really what we are seeing played out in the Fundamentalist-Modernist Controversy: Christ struggling against Satan. We are not going that far back, however. We will go to the beginning of the twentieth century, dipping occasionally into the end of the nineteenth.

This is important for us to consider because this controversy eventually gave birth to the Bible Presbyterian Church. At the same time, it is important for us to see that this is a continuation of orthodox, Bible-believing history, so that we can understand that the Bible Presbyterian Church did not simply come into existence in 1937–38 out of nowhere — it is a continuation of the true church established in the Abrahamic covenant. We firmly stand on the doctrine of the apostles. In that sense, we are one with the apostolic church.

What gave birth to the Fundamentalist-Modernist Controversy? I think we can trace it back to a desire to broaden the Presbyterian Church — a desire to make the church large, to have influence over the world, rather than simply faithfully ministering the gospel of Jesus Christ. To that end, doctrinal standards were loosened and there was an alarming growth of toleration toward modernism.

We can trace this back to 1903 when the Westminster Confession was amended to soften its Calvinist position. A chapter was added on the Holy Spirit and a chapter on the love of God. This was done in order to woo the Cumberland Presbyterian Church into the northern Presbyterian Church, so that the church could expand its borders and become more relevant. And this quest for relevancy is what eventually caused the northern Presbyterian Church to die to the gospel of Jesus Christ — and in its death to give birth to the Bible Presbyterian Church.

## The Five Fundamentals and The Fundamentals (1910)

Both terms — "modernism" and "fundamentalism" — were first used in the Presbyterian Church, all the way back in 1909. By 1910, there was already great controversy in the church.

In 1910 there was published a two-volume work called *The Fundamentals: A Testimony to the Truth*. These two volumes included 31 contributors and 90 different articles — if you search for it you will find a who's who list of conservative, Bible-believing preachers of the time. Interestingly, the cost of publishing and disseminating two million sets was borne by two Presbyterian brothers, Lyman and Milton Stewart from Los Angeles.

This was part of an attempt to establish a fundamentalist movement within the Presbyterian Church — a movement that said: stick to these fundamentals. The emphasis was on five points. These five points of fundamentalism were not intended to be exhaustive doctrinal statements or exhaustive confessions of faith, but they were the reaction of the Bible-believing conservative branch of the church to what was happening:

1. The inerrancy of the Scriptures — the Scriptures are free from error, what European scholars often called the infallibility of the Scriptures.
2. The virgin birth and deity of Christ — not something open to debate.
3. The doctrine of the substitutionary atonement, received through God's grace and human faith.
4. The bodily resurrection of Jesus.
5. The authenticity of Christ's miracles — Christ actually performed them.

These are the five fundamentals that the conservatives in the northern Presbyterian Church rallied around. Even these were reactions to rumblings within the church that dated back to the trial of Charles Briggs in the late 1890s — Briggs was a professor at Union Seminary, found guilty of heterodox doctrine and excommunicated from the denomination, but not removed from the seminary, where he continued to influence future Presbyterian ministers.

This group, now spurred on by the Stewart brothers, called for prayer bands to meet and pray, and the General Assembly of the Presbyterian Church considered these fundamentals and determined them to be essential doctrines. They were reaffirmed several times in the General Assembly, beginning in 1910, then again in 1916, and again in 1923. A minister or elder of the Presbyterian Church in the USA could not be ordained unless he believed these things.

## Fosdick's Challenge and the Auburn Affirmation (1922–1924)

Because of these reaffirmations, until 1922, liberals were not openly public about their views. They were willing to discuss their positions in back rooms, but they did not boldly declare them in public. That changed in 1922, when brazen liberalism came out into the open.

The catalyst was a sermon preached by Harry Emerson Fosdick in May of 1922, titled "Shall the Fundamentalists Win?" He preached this sermon at First Presbyterian Church in New York — though he was himself a Baptist serving as a guest preacher there. This was a reaction to another sermon that had called the fundamentalists to rise and fight. So 1922 marks the outing of modernism — modernism no longer afraid to speak publicly and loudly.

In 1923, the General Assembly again voted to reaffirm the five fundamentals — but by the narrowest of margins. Forty-five percent of all the votes went against reaffirming those five points as necessary beliefs for pastors and elders. Emboldened by that weak reaffirmation, a group of 1,274 pastors met in Auburn, New York, and signed a document known as the **Auburn Affirmation**. In it they professed allegiance to the Westminster Confession of Faith — and yet in the same document claimed that the five fundamentals were only "theories of interpretation" and that there were other, in their opinion better, ways to understand what the Bible said about the virgin birth, the inspiration of Scripture, the miracles of Christ, the resurrection, or the substitutionary atonement. They also denied the General Assembly's right to establish any essentials for ordination beyond those already in the church's constitution.

## The Committee of 1925 and the Collapse of the Standards

In 1925, a committee was established to investigate the propriety of requiring that elders and ministers believe in the five fundamentals in order to remain in or be ordained to the church. This committee was headed by broad-churchmen — men who wanted to keep the church as inclusive as possible. Bear in mind that the majority of the church was not theologically liberal. The majority was moderate — they believed the Bible, but they also believed that the church would be better off living in peace with the liberals, keeping them in the church so that the denomination would be more relevant.

In 1927, the committee reported to the General Assembly, and the Assembly decided that it could not establish essential and necessary articles of belief beyond those in the Church's constitution — and the five fundamentals were not explicitly outlined in the constitution. The result was that the inclusive position of tolerance articulated in the Auburn Affirmation became the official position of the church. Men no longer had to believe these things in order to become or remain pastors and elders.

## Decline in Foreign Missions

At the same time, there was a corresponding decline in foreign missions. Robert Speer served for decades as General Secretary of the Mission Board — essentially the head of Presbyterian missions to the world. As early as 1921, Speer — who himself appeared to hold orthodox theology — began sweeping under the rug charges of missionary heresy coming from the field.

By 1932, a commission was formed to investigate. Its report was a document called *Re-Thinking Missions*. This international commission found that missions had become essentially a syncretistic blending of Christianity with the world's religions. The Presbyterian Foreign Mission Board then tolerated — even promoted — figures like Pearl Buck, a Presbyterian missionary in China who genuinely believed that the worst thing one could do for the Chinese was preach Christ to them.

Ultimately, a gospel-less utilitarianism in missions led to intolerance for the conservative minority. Once the denomination adopted the view that all views must be accepted in order to keep the church broad, the only view that became unacceptable was the exclusive view of the conservatives. The conservatives were pressured to keep quiet.

## The Fall of Princeton Seminary

At the same time, there was a weakening of educational institutions — especially Princeton Seminary, which had been the last bastion of Presbyterian orthodoxy. As early as 1909, student rebellion was taking place, with students objecting to what they perceived as impractical intellectualism.

As early as 1914 — ten years before the Auburn Affirmation — there were already moves to wrest Princeton from the conservatives through the appointment of J. Ross Stevenson as president. Stevenson replaced Francis Patton. Patton had represented the old position: modernism is not to be tolerated. Stevenson himself was not a modernist, but he believed modernists should be tolerated — and so began the softening of Princeton's leadership.

By 1929, Princeton's governance was reorganized by the General Assembly. Princeton had from its founding had two boards: a board of trustees, charged with property and physical buildings; and a board of directors, charged with curriculum, student life, and faculty hiring — and that board had remained conservative. Slowly, the board of trustees was filled with men who had signed the Auburn Affirmation. In 1929, the General Assembly abolished the board of directors and merged the two boards. Now the board of trustees — populated by Auburn Affirmation signers — was in charge of curriculum, hiring professors, and student life.

## J. Gresham Machen and Westminster Theological Seminary

This was the context in which J. Gresham Machen operated. I will not say much about him since Elder Nick Anderson will cover him more fully. Machen was born in 1881 and died in 1937. The one thing I will say is: you must read his book *Christianity and Liberalism*, which he wrote in 1923. It is as relevant today as it was then.

With the boards at Princeton reorganized, it was clear to the conservatives that they needed their own institution. In 1929, Machen founded Westminster Theological Seminary. He brought with him conservative faculty members from Princeton.

The early years of Westminster were turbulent. In 1935–36, the majority of the Westminster board was unwilling to separate from the old denomination. The majority of those board members resigned and followed O. T. Allis — the celebrated Old Testament scholar — away from Westminster Theological Seminary. In 1937, another major blow came with the departure of Dr. Allan MacRae, who resigned to lead the newly founded Faith Theological Seminary.

## The Independent Board for Presbyterian Foreign Missions (1933)

In 1933, following the bad reports from the mission field and the acceptance of a false gospel in Presbyterian foreign missions, Machen and other men founded the **Independent Board for Presbyterian Foreign Missions**. As a side note, that board is still an independent agency of the Bible Presbyterian Church to this day.

After several overtures to reform the official foreign mission board failed or were ignored, these men established the Independent Board in 1933. Up to that point, Machen and his associates had been viewed as a nuisance in the church — like a small pebble in your shoe: annoying but not enough to stop walking. But when the Independent Board was established, money began leaving the denomination. And money talks louder than anything else.

The General Assembly in 1934 declared: either disassociate from the Independent Board and dissolve it, or you will be defraught. To be defraught is to lose your ordination — the equivalent for a minister or elder of excommunication. They were charged with breaking the Fifth Commandment for not obeying the General Assembly. Obedience to constitutional powers was made equivalent to obedience to Christ.

Because these men refused to submit, the Presbyterian Church in the USA purged itself of its conscience. J. Gresham Machen, Carl McIntyre, Oliver Buswell, Roy Brumbaugh, H. S. Laird, Paul Woolley, and others all left the denomination. Roy Brumbaugh was my wife's grandfather, who was defraught by the Olympia Presbytery right here in our area.

## The Presbyterian Church of America (1936)

Before leaving, these men established what they called a Constitutional Covenant Union — an alliance of conservatives attempting internal reform. They were trying, like Luther, to reform the church from within. But they also understood that if all else failed, this union would become a denomination that would stand as the true spiritual succession of the old Presbyterian church. They had an official publication, the *Presbyterian Guardian*, established to counter the newly formed *Christianity Today*.

When leaving became inevitable, these men did not leave willingly — they were forced out. Dr. Machen fought through every level of church government and was found in the wrong at every level before he would leave. But when leaving became inevitable, they established the **Presbyterian Church of America** on June 11, 1936. This is not to be confused with the current PCA — the Presbyterian Church in America. This was the original Presbyterian Church of America.

At that point they declared the Presbyterian Church in the USA officially apostate, and they adopted the Westminster Confession and the church's constitution of 1934 — with the proviso that the 1903 amendments (those extra chapters added to broaden the church) would be dropped.

I came across a brief article by Dr. Oliver Buswell — one of the men who left in 1936 — published in the *Christian Beacon* in June 1952. Buswell wrote: "In 1903, the Presbyterian Church in the United States of America adopted certain amendments in order to please groups which were doctrinally weak and poorly instructed" — referring to the Cumberland Presbyterian Church. He noted that Dr. Benjamin Warfield, one of the greatest Calvinist theologians of the past generation, strongly protested against those amendments, but when they were adopted, Warfield declared — as Dr. J. Gresham Machen related to him — that these amendments, "weak and misleading as they were, actually changed the system of doctrine." And so our forefathers decided to remove them from our adopted constitution.

## The Death of Machen and the Division That Followed

In God's providence, Machen died on January 1, 1937. Machen had a policy never to turn down a speaking engagement if he was available, and so he went to North Dakota in December to preach. He was already ill when he departed, became very sick, and died on January 1, 1937. The official cause was pneumonia, though I have suggested he may have died of a broken heart from all the fighting taking place in the newly formed group.

With Machen's death, there was a vacuum of strong, diplomatic leadership, and divisions in the new denomination became apparent. Two groups were vying for dominance:

**The Old School majority** — a technical term referring to a way of thinking about missions and revivalism in the Presbyterian Church going back to the 1800s. These were strict confessionalists who insisted on the European Reformed heritage and had their roots in Princeton.

**The New School minority** — these men subscribed to simple biblical legalism. They were more concerned with historical-grammatical interpretation of Scripture than with allegorism. They thought of themselves as American Presbyterians — more nationalistic, patriotic, in favor of a less centralized and more democratic church, with a greater emphasis on personal piety. They also identified with the broader fundamentalist movement and had roots not only in Princeton but also in the late nineteenth-century Bible conference movement, especially those focused on prophecy.

## Three Issues That Caused the Final Division

Three major stated issues eventually separated the group that became the Bible Presbyterian Church from the original Presbyterian Church of America:

**1. Eschatology.** Some say the Bible Presbyterian Church was founded so that everyone could be premillennial — but that is not quite the case. The Bible Presbyterian Church was not founded to *force* premillennialism on anyone. In fact, it was the opposite. The Bible Presbyterian Church was founded to provide eschatological *liberty*. The premillennialists in the Presbyterian Church of America were being pressured by amillennialists who were trying to enforce amillennialism on everyone. The new group wanted to protect people's freedom of eschatology. We can see this in resolutions from our first Synod. The constitution was amended to *allow* for a premillennial return of Christ — this was done to protect, not to exclude.

**2. Total abstinence from alcohol.** The group that became the Bible Presbyterian Church stood on the side of total abstinence.

**3. Independent boards.** Should the denomination be controlled by its own boards of missions and education, or should the denomination approve independent agencies for those purposes? The argument for independent agencies was that if the denomination ever drifts into apostasy, the agencies — being self-perpetuating and not appointable by the denomination — would remain faithful.

These three issues caused the final separation. The Bible Presbyterian Church formally constituted in 1937–38, with our first Synod taking place in 1938. As a footnote: the Presbyterian Church of America was eventually sued by the Presbyterian Church in the USA, which claimed the name was too similar and would cause confusion. They settled that lawsuit and changed their name to the **Orthodox Presbyterian Church**.

## The Bible Presbyterian Church: A History of Splits and Growth

In some ways, our history can be told by the splits we have gone through. We started as a strong church with great promise, strong evangelistic drive, and a genuine commitment to missions, church planting, and cultural engagement. We were part of the fundamentalist movement, but we were an unusual fundamentalist church — very engaged with culture, very committed to changing society, which is not the bunker mentality you typically see in fundamentalism.

After our founding, however, we did go through cycles of division:

- **1956** — The most costly split. A large group of men left the Bible Presbyterian Church and formed the Evangelical Presbyterian Church. Men like Francis Schaeffer (the very first man ordained in the Bible Presbyterian Church), Oliver Buswell, Robert C. Rayburn, and Jay Adams all left in 1956. That denomination eventually joined the PCA, which is why most of the longstanding northern churches in the PCA were originally Bible Presbyterian churches and still bear that heritage.

- **1971** — Dr. McIntyre persuaded the board of Faith Theological Seminary to force Dr. McRae into early retirement. In reaction, Dr. McRae and the entire faculty left Faith Theological Seminary and established Biblical Theological Seminary in Philadelphia.

- **1976** — A small group formed the American Presbyterian Church out of the Bible Presbyterian Church, consisting of perhaps one to three congregations, over what they held was a hyper-application of the regulative principle of worship.

- **1984** — Another group left over financial issues and the denomination's association with the International Council of Christian Churches. Following that split, a particularly bitter defamation campaign was launched by those who left, running the names of many of our men through the mud.

- **2008** — Five churches along the eastern seaboard left the Synod over the Synod's relationship with the Orthodox Presbyterian Church, and formed Faith Presbytery.

## Who We Are Today

Today we are a denomination that is growing and faithful to the Scriptures. In one way or another, the 1984 split shifted our attention back to our own confession and constitution, and through the leadership of men like Robert Anderson, John Battle, Blan Rogers, and James Blizzard, we have become much more confessional and Presbyterian than we were before 1984.

We are made up of four presbyteries with 27 churches plus a few church plants. We have four approved synod-accredited agencies: Westminster Reformed Seminary, the Presbyterian Missionary Union, and the Independent Board for Presbyterian Foreign Missions, with Bible Presbyterian Publications currently on hiatus. We are present in four international mission fields: Myanmar, Cambodia, Brazil, and Lebanon — and we are actively planting churches.

When people ask me about the Bible Presbyterian Church, I see it as a church of opportunities — a church where things are happening, where we have the foundational structure to disciple nations and the people willing to do it. We are a church intent on glorifying God and contending for Jesus Christ. That is who we are today.

And all of it is grounded in the Protestant Reformation of the sixteenth century, flowing through the Fundamentalist-Modernist Controversy of the twentieth century, pressing on into the twenty-first century — and Lord willing, into centuries to come — in service of Christ: a Reformed, confessional, evangelism-driven, missions-oriented denomination. We are thankful to the Lord for establishing the Bible Presbyterian Church and keeping it faithful to him through the decades.
